title: Academic Withdrawal Refunds
content:
Withdrawal and Refund Policies
Morgan State University

---

TUITION REFUND SCHEDULE

The following refund schedule applies when students officially withdraw from the University:

Time of Withdrawal | Tuition Refund | Fees Refund
-------------------|----------------|-------------
Before classes begin | 100% | 100%
During first week | 100% | 0%
During second week | 80% | 0%
Between two and three weeks | 60% | 0%
Between three and four weeks | 40% | 0%
Between four and five weeks | 20% | 0%
After five weeks | 0% | 0%

---

MANDATORY FEES

Note: Mandatory fees are non-refundable once instruction begins.

Mandatory Fees Include:
- Student Activity Fee
- Athletic Fee
- Student Union Operating Fee
- Student Union Auxiliary Fee
- Insurance Fee

---

IMPORTANT WITHDRAWAL INFORMATION

To receive any refund or adjustment, you MUST officially withdraw from the University.

IMPORTANT: Simply not attending class does NOT count as a withdrawal.

How to Officially Withdraw:
1. Contact the Office of Records and Registration
2. Complete the official withdrawal process
3. Return any university property
4. Settle any outstanding balances

---

ADDITIONAL REFUND INFORMATION

For Room & Board Refunds:
Contact the Office of Residence Life

For Changes to Financial Aid Package:
Contact the Office of Financial Aid

To Check Your Refund Status:
Visit bursar.morgan.edu

---

FINANCIAL AID IMPACT

Withdrawing from courses may affect your financial aid:
- May need to return a portion of federal aid
- May affect Satisfactory Academic Progress (SAP)
- May impact future aid eligibility

Contact the Office of Financial Aid before withdrawing to understand the impact.

---

CONTACT INFORMATION

Office of Records and Registration
Office of Residence Life
Office of Financial Aid
Bursar's Office: bursar.morgan.edu

title: Academic 4Plus1 Accelerated Masters
content:
Special Programs
Morgan State University - Computer Science Department

4+1 ACCELERATED MASTER'S PROGRAM

Overview:
The 4+1 program allows undergraduate students to complete both their Bachelor's and Master's degrees in Computer Science in just 5 years (4 years undergraduate + 1 year graduate).

How It Works:
- Instead of taking some 400-level COSC/CLCO classes, take the corresponding 500-level graduate classes that are already "crosslisted" with the courses
- Graduate credits earned during undergraduate years count toward both degrees
- Saves time and money compared to traditional path

Benefits:
- Complete MS in just one additional year
- Begin graduate coursework while still an undergraduate
- Seamless transition from BS to MS
- Cost savings on graduate tuition

Eligibility:
- Contact your academic advisor for eligibility requirements
- Generally requires strong GPA
- Must meet graduate admission requirements

For details and questions, contact your academic advisor.

title: Academic Advising Procedures
content:
ACADEMIC ADVISING PROCEDURES - MORGAN STATE COMPUTER SCIENCE DEPARTMENT

DEGREE WORKS:
Degree Works is an online advising audit tool that helps you track your progress towards graduation.
Link: https://www.morgan.edu/registrar/degreeworks

FORMS AND PROCEDURES:

Permission to Take Courses Off-Campus:
Request to take courses off-campus. Video tutorial: https://youtu.be/4LlY9yyD9o0
Policy:
- Off-campus course enrollment is restricted with a few exceptions
- You must submit the official form on the Registrar's website
- Core Computer Science courses must be taken on campus and are NOT eligible for off-campus credit
- Seniors are generally NOT allowed to take courses off-campus except under very specific circumstances - please discuss with the department chair directly
- Non-seniors may pursue an articulation agreement with Morgan State University for off-campus courses

Change of Major Form:
Request to change your major, minor, or concentration.
Video tutorial: https://youtu.be/Qjl2h9Z6SGo?si=EuQ8pWY8cqa08f2Q

Excess Credit Request Form:
Request to take additional credits for the semester.
Link: https://www.morgan.edu/office-of-the-registrar/priority-registration-time-ticket-groupings

Withdrawal/Cancellation Form:
Available via WebSIS Student Self-Service.
Instructions: https://www.morgan.edu/Documents/ADMINISTRATION/OFFICES/registrar/Student%20Communication.pdf

Course Registration Override:
Contact Ms. Wendy Smith at Wendy.Smith@morgan.edu
Override requests must be submitted to the academic department
Overrides are not guaranteed - department chairperson's decision is final

How to Access Registrar Forms:
1. Go to WebSIS Student Self-Service and click 'Service Requests'
2. Enter your Morgan State University email and click 'Continue'
3. Do NOT click 'Sign in with Google', 'Microsoft', or 'Apple'
4. Launch the Dashboard

Offered: Fall/Spring
Description: Introduction to programming concepts and problem-solving using a high-level programming language.

COSC 112 - Introduction to Computer Science II
Credits: 4
Prerequisite: COSC 111 with a grade of "C" or higher

Description: Continuation of COSC 111, covering advanced programming concepts and data structures.

Description: Ethical issues in computing and professional responsibility.

Offered: Fall/Spring
Description: Study of fundamental data structures and algorithms for efficient problem-solving.

COSC 241 - Computer Systems and Digital Logic
Credits: 3
Prerequisite: COSC 112 and MATH 241 with grades of "C" or higher, or departmental permission
Offered: Fall/Spring
Description: Introduction to computer organization and digital logic design.

COSC 281 - Discrete Structures
Credits: 3
Prerequisite: COSC 112 with a grade of "C" or higher
Offered: Fall/Spring
Description: Mathematical foundations for computer science including logic, sets, and combinatorics.

COSC 349 - Computer Networks
Credits: 3

Offered: Fall/Spring
Description: Fundamentals of computer networking, protocols, and network architecture.

COSC 351 - Cybersecurity

Description: Introduction to cybersecurity principles and practices.

COSC 352 - Organization of Programming Languages

Description: Study of programming language concepts, paradigms, and design principles.

COSC 354 - Operating Systems

Description: Principles of operating system design and implementation.

COSC 458 - Software Engineering

COSC 459 - Database Design
Credits: 3

title: Academic Degrees
content:
MORGAN STATE UNIVERSITY COMPUTER SCIENCE DEGREE PROGRAMS

BACHELOR OF SCIENCE (B.S.) IN COMPUTER SCIENCE
Total Credits: 120 (44 general education, 11 supporting, 65 major)
Requirements:
- Cumulative GPA of 2.0 or better
- Major GPA of 2.0 or better
- No grades below C in the major
- Pass Senior Departmental Comprehensive Examination
- Six credits in Complementary Studies Program
Website: https://catalog.morgan.edu/preview_program.php?catoid=26&poid=5968&returnto=1880

BACHELOR OF SCIENCE (B.S.) IN CLOUD COMPUTING
Total Credits: 120

1. Data Structures to Master:
   - Arrays and Strings
   - Linked Lists
   - Trees and Graphs

   - Stacks and Queues
   - Heaps

title: Career Internships Overview
content:
Internship Guide for CS Students - Overview
Morgan State University

---

WHAT IS AN INTERNSHIP?
An internship is a hands-on learning experience that blends classroom knowledge and theory with real-world application and skill-building in a professional environment, whether in-person, remote, or hybrid. It offers students a chance to acquire practical experience, build professional networks, explore career paths, and establish connections in their chosen fields. Additionally, internships act as a key hiring tool for employers, allowing them to mentor and assess potential employees and serves as a significant recruiting mechanism for employers.

---

BENEFITS OF PARTICIPATING IN INTERNSHIPS

- Career Exploration: Internships shed light on specific industries or roles, helping students pinpoint their interests, strengths, and career goals, ultimately aiding in smarter career decisions.

- Competitive Edge: In a crowded job market, prior work experience sets you apart. Completing an internship enhances your appeal to employers after graduation.

- Confidence Building: Finishing an internship successfully boosts self-assurance, offering a sense of achievement and affirming your capabilities in a professional context.

- Hands-on Experience: Internships bring classroom lessons to life, allowing students to apply theoretical concepts to real-world tasks, deepening their expertise and skills.

- Networking Opportunities: By connecting with industry professionals, internships expand your contacts, opening doors to mentorships, job prospects, and lasting career relationships.

- Potential Job Offers: Per the National Association of Colleges and Employers (NACE), about 56% of interns historically receive full-time job offers, as employers favor candidates proven to fit their organization.

- Professional Development: Exposure to workplace culture, etiquette, and dynamics during internships sharpens professionalism, adaptability, and awareness of job expectations.

- References and Recommendations: Strong endorsements from internship supervisors or mentors can bolster job or graduate school applications.

- Resume Building: Internships enrich your resume with tangible experience, showcasing dedication and initiative to future employers.

- Skill Development: Internships refine a range of abilities—technical know-how, communication, problem-solving, teamwork, time management, and adaptability—essential for career success.

---

TYPES OF INTERNSHIPS

- Academic-Based Internships: Required by some programs for course credit, these involve practical work tied to your major, with performance assessed by your school.

- Co-op Programs: These alternate between academic terms and extended work periods in relevant fields, often spanning multiple semesters in a structured format.

- International Internships: Held overseas, these provide global industry exposure, cross-cultural immersion, and adaptability to diverse work settings and languages.

- Micro-Internships: Short-term and project-focused, lasting days to weeks, these offer quick practical experience on specific tasks. Learn more via Parker Dewey.

- Paid Internships: These compensate interns financially, combining valuable experience with income to offset living costs, making them highly desirable.

- Research Internships: Found in labs or research bodies, these focus on scientific exploration in fields like biology, chemistry, physics, or engineering.

- Summer Internships: Occurring during summer break, these range from weeks to months, delivering intensive work experience in a condensed time frame.

- Unpaid Internships: Offering no pay but rich experience, these are common in nonprofits, government, or small firms with tight budgets.

- Virtual/Remote Internships: Growing since the COVID-19 shift, these allow remote work on tasks and projects without an office presence.

title: Financial Federal Work Study
content:
Federal Work Study Program (FWSP)
Morgan State University

---

PROGRAM OVERVIEW

The Federal Work Study Program at Morgan State University (MSU) offers financial assistance through part-time employment opportunities. This federal program supports students by providing on- and off-campus jobs during and between enrollment periods. These positions are designed to fit students' schedules, align with their educational programs or vocational goals, and are available through nonprofit or for-profit employers for currently enrolled students.

The Federal Work Study Office serves as a referral hub, connecting undergraduate students to both federal and non-federal job opportunities in the community.

---

JOB OPPORTUNITIES

On-Campus Jobs:
- Students can apply to departments of their choice
- Must complete an interview process for consideration
- Jobs are designed to work around class schedules

Off-Campus or Other Assignments:
- Students can visit the Work Study Office in person for referrals to available openings
- Departments notify the office of vacancies
- Final hiring decisions rest with the department

---

ELIGIBILITY REQUIREMENTS

Eligibility is determined based on federal regulations and a confidential review of a student's financial need:
- Must be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident
- Must be enrolled as a matriculated student at MSU
- Must demonstrate financial need as determined by FAFSA
- Must be making Satisfactory Academic Progress (SAP)

---

HOW TO APPLY

1. Complete the FAFSA (Free Application for Federal Student Aid)
2. Indicate interest in work-study on FAFSA
3. If awarded work-study, search for available positions
4. Apply and interview for positions of interest
5. Complete all required employment paperwork

---

FEDERAL WORK STUDY OFFICE CONTACT

Email: msuworkstudy@morgan.edu
Phone: 443-885-3141
Fax: 443-885-8334
Office Hours: Monday-Friday, 8:30 AM - 5:00 PM

title: Financial Scholarships
content:
Scholarships at Morgan State University

---

SCHOLARSHIPUNIVERSE

Students can apply for scholarships and grants through the University's scholarship platform, ScholarshipUniverse (SU). This online platform helps match students with relevant, vetted external scholarship opportunities, as well as internal scholarships offered by the university.

Website: https://morgan.scholarshipuniverse.com/

Important Notes:
- Students who receive the Bozeman, Quinn, or Jenkins awards are not eligible for the Special Assistance awards available through SU
- They may qualify for other awards
- Need-based funds may be reduced or canceled if students receive other institutional aid that exceeds their actual costs

---

INSTITUTIONAL SCHOLARSHIPS

These awards are available on a limited basis to new and continuing Morgan students:
- Based on need and academic average
- Award amounts vary
- Not guaranteed

---

SCHOLARSHIP ACCEPTANCE POLICY

Students awarded full academic, institutional, or athletic scholarships are NOT eligible to receive other MSU institutional awards.

If multiple full awards are offered from separate MSU offices, programs, and/or committees, students will be required to select only ONE award offer.

---

AWARD PAYMENT AND OVERAWARDS

Your total combined financial aid cannot exceed your MSU Cost of Attendance budget. This includes:
- Academic/merit awards
- Academic grants and scholarships
- University need-based awards
- All other aid from all sources (government, MSU, and private)

If Total Aid Exceeds Cost of Attendance:
1. If all University-funded awards, funding will be adjusted
2. Reduction order: Loans first, then University funding, then outside funds

---

OVERAWARD INFORMATION

Overawards usually result from:
- Student receiving aid the Financial Aid Office was not aware of
- Application errors by the student or Financial Aid Office

Student Responsibility:
- Provide written documentation of additional resources to the Financial Aid Office
- Do this as soon as you become aware of additional aid
- All sources of aid must be accounted for, even if not processed through the office

Important: We are bound by Federal regulation to correct overawards, which may result in:
- Funds being returned to an aid program
- Student owing the university money
- No exceptions can be made

---

COST OF ATTENDANCE

View information on Cost of Attendance on the Morgan State University Financial Aid website.

title: Financial Tax Information
content:
Tax Information
Morgan State University

---

1098-T TAX CREDIT FORM

Under the Tax Relief Act of 1997, the 1098-T form provides information to help determine eligibility for the Hope Scholarship or Lifetime Learning Credits.

When You'll Receive It:
- Mailed to your permanent address by January 31 each year
- If not received, download a copy at: https://tra.maximus.com/

First-Time Users:
- Click "First Time Users" on the website
- Complete the registration process

Recommendation: Consult a tax advisor to determine your qualified amount.

---

FORM DETAILS

Box 1: Total payments received by Morgan State for qualified tuition and related expenses during the calendar year.
Note: This may not match total charges paid, as not all expenses (e.g., room and board) qualify per IRS rules.

Box 2: No longer used starting in 2018. Previously (2017 and earlier), it showed qualified tuition and mandatory fees billed. Room and board are not qualified expenses.

Box 4: Scholarships and grants (e.g., Pell Grant, SEOG, EAG) received in the calendar year.

Box 5: Grants, scholarships, and third-party benefits received (e.g., federal/state grants). Loans are not included.

Box 8: Indicates half-time student status.

Box 9: Indicates graduate student status.

---

INTERNATIONAL STUDENTS

If classified as an international student, you will NOT receive a 1098-T form.

---

CORRECTING THE 1098-T

If your Social Security Number (SSN) is incorrect:
1. Submit a picture ID and a copy of your SSN card to the Office of Records & Registration for correction.
2. After confirmation, submit a written request for a revised 1098-T to the Office of the Bursar.

---

WHO WON'T RECEIVE A 1098-T?

You will not receive a form if:
- Your courses offer no academic credit, even if enrolled in a degree program
- You're a nonresident alien with no SSN, SIN, or TIN on file
- Your qualified tuition and related expenses are fully waived or covered by grants, scholarships, or third-party benefits

---

ADDITIONAL RESOURCES

For more on student loan interest deductions, Lifetime Learning Credit, and Hope Credits:
- Visit www.irs.gov
- Review IRS Publication 970 (Tax Benefits for Higher Education)
- Review Publication 520 (Scholarships and Fellowships)
- Available online or by calling 1-800-TAX-FORM

content:

SATISFACTORY ACADEMIC PROGRESS (SAP)

To maintain financial aid eligibility, students must meet SAP requirements:

1. GPA Requirement: Maintain minimum cumulative GPA
   - Undergraduate: 2.0
   - Graduate: 3.0

2. Completion Rate: Complete at least 67% of attempted credit hours

3. Maximum Timeframe: Complete degree within 150% of program length
   - For 120-credit program: Maximum 180 attempted credits

SAP is reviewed at the end of each semester. Students not meeting requirements may:
- Lose financial aid eligibility
- Be placed on Financial Aid Warning or Suspension

title: General Peer Bears Mentoring
content:
Peer BEARS Mentoring Program
Morgan State University - School of Computer, Mathematical, and Natural Sciences

The Peer B.E.A.R.S. (Bringing Enrichment and Rearing Success) Program showcases the talents and passions of upperclassmen in the SCMNS who mentor first-year students. The program aims to enhance student engagement and persistence through graduation by guiding freshmen as they navigate a new environment, rigorous coursework, and independence. This peer mentoring initiative focuses on improving study habits, science and math competency, and professional development through academic, professional, and social activities.

MENTORS
Peer BEARS mentors are upperclassmen (sophomores, juniors, seniors) who are certified College Reading and Learning Association (CRLA) Level 1 Peer Educators. They provide support to freshmen SCMNS majors throughout their first year.

Interested in joining the Peer BEARS mentor team?
Email: SCMNS-PeerBEARS@morgan.edu

MENTEES
Mentees are freshmen with majors in SCMNS, mentored throughout their first year. To apply, complete the mentee application form. After review, our Student Success Coordinator will contact you if a mentor is assigned.

How to Apply: Complete the mentee application form available through the SCMNS department.

The Writing Center supports undergraduate and graduate students across all disciplines to improve their writing and communication skills. The center offers free tutoring services for a variety of writing assignments, from first-year papers to graduate theses.
